data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: The MLP To Buy Before 2026
data.description:
Introduction

A lot has happened for Enterprise Products Partners (NYSE: EPD) since my last analysis on the company and I was actually surprised that I did not review my thesis earlier. Since my last analysis, the company has appreciated by a total return of around 7%, actually making it an outperformer in the midstream industry.

With the slew of earnings and strategic deals that the company has done, I want to assess if my Strong Buy case is still relevant with the current O&G dynamics and if it is still the safest bet in the industry.

Current Dynamics

I'll first dive into the most recent earnings with EPD actually reporting a mixed set, with a beat on revenue at $12.02b while EPS missed by nearly 9% at 61 cents. And though the company missed on earnings, I believe that the underlying operational volumes were relatively strong in a similar fashion to other midstream companies such as Energy Transfer. EPD set nine new operational records thanks to strong demand across its vast integrated system primarily in natural gas processing and NGL pipeline volumes, which increased by around 9% Y/Y. There were some slight headwinds, mainly lower sales margins in the NGL market as well as some downtime, but overall, the quarter was sound and was overly positive on the company's outlook.

This has pushed EPD to boost its quarterly distribution to around $0.545 per common unit, which is an increase of 3.8% Y/Y. Furthermore, the DCF coverage ratio is relatively healthy at around 1.5x thanks to a total DCF of $1.8B. The company also increased its buyback authorization to $5B, up from an initial $2B. And on a reverse note, EPD reduced its CapEx guidance for 2026 to between $2.2B and $2.5B, which is a 50% reduction compared to 2025. This is mainly due to the company actually signalling the end of its multi-year construction cycle. The direct consequence should be a drastic increase in the partnership's FCF, as stated by A.J. Teague, the company's Co-CEO. Thus, coupled with the record volumes, we should see a great 2026 for the company's profitability.

On another note, I feel like EPD has been highly focused on asserting its dominance over the Permian's NGL and Natural gas value chain as it made some strategic acquisitions and major JV expansions. First, the Oxy acquisition and processing deal directly improved EPD's position in the Midland Basin. The deal came at a value of around $580MM in cash to acquire some natural gas gathering systems and around 200 miles of Nat gas gathering pipelines. The rationale behind the deal would be that it supports Oxy's production and allows EPD to have access to around 1,000 drillable locations, as well as give long-term contract agreements between the two major energy players. Following this, EPD has announced that it will build a new Athena natural gas processing plant to handle the increased volume from the deal.

Furthermore, EPD and ExxonMobil just recently announced that they will sign a partnership agreement for EPD's Bahia Natural Gas Liquids NGL Pipeline, in which Exxon will acquire around 40% of the project. This will translate into a contribution of around $650MM to cover its proportional share of the costs incurred to date on the 550-mile pipeline that is currently being commissioned to transport 600,000 barrels per day of mixed NGLs from the Permian to EPD's fractionation hub. In all, I believe that this was a good deal as it derisks the project for EPD and secures a massive anchor shipper in Exxon Mobil, thus immediately greenlighting an expansion that will capture a projected 30%+ growth in the Permian NGL production by 2030.

Valuation

Currently, EPD has an EV/EBITDA of 11.14, one of the lowest among its peers, beaten only by one of my holdings, Energy Transfer (ET). With the investment cycle being finished, I expect EBIDTA to pick up in 2026 and probably reach over $11B based on the current volume throughput. I also expect the company's FCF yield to pick up as the company tones down CapEx, raising the current yield of 4.4%, making the company even more interesting on a valuation standpoint. And though generally EPD has been pricier thanks to its secure operation in general, mainly compared to peers, we can see that there is a sort of discount currently for EPD, offering a buying opportunity for income investors. Mainly, the current distribution yield stands at 6.64% and should drastically rise in the coming years as EPD reduces its CapEx, thus increasing Distributable Cash Flow.

On the growth front, with the company's capital being heavily employed in the Permian and the Gulf Coast export capacity, I believe that the company's future is heavily tied to the highest-growth regions in North America. EPD has become a dominant player in the Permian, mainly in NGL and gas processing, which should continue to have a significant importance as transition fuel. EPD is clearly cementing its NGL domination with ongoing strong demand going through its Mont Belvieu facility, which is undergoing further expansion that should well tie into the durable EBITDA growth as it comes online. I'll say that despite EPD being known for its stability, right now could be a great moment for its growth prospects as well.

Though margins have been under relative pressure over the past couple of quarters, they remain highly stable thanks to the business model and EBITDA margin is picking back up towards 20% as fee-based contracts are currently accounting for around 82% of sales for the past 9 months. As I mentioned earlier, I expect margins and overall profitability to pick back up for Enterprise, with, for example, the Fractionator 14 ramping up, boosting processing margins for the remainder of the year and into 2026. Though the core margin growth will come from the $5.1B realized in growth projects coming online, that should push EPD's profitability back in line with peers. Though right now, EPD has an excellent ROE, higher than Kinder Morgan or even Enbridge at 19.7% thanks to the company's highly efficient operation.

Risks

There is always the risk tied to producers that even with EPD's operation being insulated from commodity price volatility, customers are not. If prices fall too drastically, producers could simply cut their production, reducing throughput through EPD's system. Though NGL marketing, processing margin, and the overall petrochemical segment are exposed to price volatility.

And though the arms race has toned down for midstream companies, there is still aggressive competition mainly to get a piece of the Permian pie and with all of that CapEx spent on the area, it could lead to excess pipeline and processing capacity across the industry.

Conclusion

At $32 a unit, I still believe that Enterprise is well worth owning in an income-focused portfolio. It is probably the most stable operation in the midstream, and the premium that you used to pay for that security blanket is currently eroded, leaving a great opportunity for investors. We should see a high uptick in profitability in 2026, pushing unit prices to new heights, in my opinion. I keep my Strong Buy rating on Enterprise Products Partners.

data.title: USA: Hold While AI Bubble Fears Loom
data.description:
Liberty All-Star Equity (USA) stood out to me because of its double-digit yield from an equity fund. With a long-term performance comparable to the S&P 500, it's not a bad investment, it's been suitable income for the past decade. Nevertheless, I think risks related to an AI bubble in its tech positions are too great without a stronger NAV discount first.

Concept of the Fund

USA is intended to be an income fund, regularly distributing dividends to shareholders. As its most recent annual report explains:

The current policy is to pay distributions on its shares totaling approximately 10 percent of its net asset value per year, payable in four quarterly installments of 2.5 percent of the Fund’s net asset value at the close of the New York Stock Exchange on the Friday prior to each quarterly declaration date.

Put in plainer terms, USA does not primarily support the distributions with underlying cash flow, such as interest or dividends. Rather, it will simply distribute a portion of its NAV, and this is only supported as long as the underlying investments perform well.

The tables above show how the portfolio is invested to achieve this. The top ten holdings reveal many large cap stocks, including some Mag 7 names.

This investment selection is a product of a five-way split in the portfolio management, which it considers three parts "value" and two parts "growth." Each manager allocates roughly an equal portion of the assets based on those value and growth preferences.

The result is a portfolio that is similar to the S&P 500 in many respects, but active selection limits the portfolio to 139 holdings, with less concentration in its top ten picks, and lower multiples to earnings and book. The portfolio dividend yield, at 1.4%, only accounts for a small portion of the distributions, meaning that assets must be trimmed each quarter to maintain the distribution.

While this is an atypical income fund, this doesn't mean it's incapable of distributing, and there are several years of results to give insights into this strategy.

Historical Results

The first thing to consider is its distribution history. Over the past decade, USA has paid dividends every year, even in the midst of COVID, rate hikes, the Russian invasion of Ukraine, and other disruptions.

The long-term trend is one of growth but with fluctuations. This can be largely explained by trends in the market.

For example, NAV per share (displayed above as book value per share), declined in 2022, as COVID stimulus halted and as the Federal Reserve began to hike rates. This necessarily reduces the size of a distribution based on a percentage of NAV. The long-term trend has been that NAV per share generally increases over time.

Most entry points in the past decade would enjoy a 10% yield on cost or more if held to the current day, indicating that it's been a fairly effective income vehicle in that regard, even if it entails trimming its capital.

More recently, the S&P 500 has outperformed USA, but there are many bouts in which USA performed better. Thus, it's not only proved to be good for straight income; it's been an effective vehicle for long-term compounding through reinvestment.

Outlook and Valuation

Recently, in addition to the high yield of 11%, USA has been trading at close to a 10% discount to NAV.

The shares often trade near NAV, and discounts like this are not too common. Considering the bearish attitude in markets recently that drove stocks, it's not surprising that USA is lower, particularly if investors expect the stock market to go down more. Treasury Secretary Bessent warned early November that a recession is likely without more rate cuts by the Fed, and many similar sentiments have been felt lately.

While USA doesn't use leverage to enhance its yield, several of their growth stocks (largely those with a tech focus) trade at large multiples, which could easily contract if market sentiment turns more negative. In October, an AI bubble bursting was most-cited risk by fund managers, indicating concerns the ultimate returns of AI capex may not match today's valuations. Equity-focused funds like USA would face a correction if such were to occur.

Take a look at the forward P/Es of their five largest tech holdings.

  • Nvidia (NVDA): 38
  • Microsoft (MSFT): 30
  • Alphabet (GOOG): 30
  • Amazon (AMZN): 33
  • Meta (META): 25

These recently accounted for 17% of the portfolio. All are priced for growth multiples, and those are very sensitive to setbacks, anything that makes the growth less than expected. An AI bubble bursting could do that.

Generally speaking, USA's total return has declined alongside the broader market in these moments.

I can perhaps see a case to reinvest dividends in an existing position at these prices, with the NAV discount especially, but taking an initial position is difficult to support when there's potentially a lot of air to be let out of the tire. I think a stronger NAV discount, as much as 20%, is warranted before an entry seems attractive.

Conclusion

USA is not a bad equity fund, and it functions well. It does not take a great deal of risks, and I think investors have the benefit of reinvesting for total returns like that of the S&P 500 or opting for straight income. I think it's just important to buy well and be mindful that a reasonably managed fund is exposed to market downside risks. For those reasons I give it a Hold rating.

data.title: Charting The Year Ahead: Investment Ideas For 2026
data.description:
Introduction

After a year of broad-based gains, many investors feel both upbeat and uncertain looking toward 2026.

Equities extended their bull run in 2025, but valuations remain historically stretched amid a sharp dispersion of returns across sectors. Cash offered both safety and income for a while, but it no longer seems attractive as the Federal Reserve cuts interest rates. And the U.S. economy appears resilient, yet a K-shaped split shows prosperity for wealthier households diverging from mounting strain for others.

We’ll update our views on the economy and fixed income markets in more detail in our January Cyclical Outlook. For now, we’ll explore select areas of interest across financial markets looking toward 2026 – individual themes that can be brought together in a portfolio – in a way that’s actionable for investors and advisors.

Equities: Expensive on the surface, value beneath

U.S. equities approach 2026 with valuations still near historical highs after a multi-year, technology-driven rally. While AI investment continues to underpin economic growth and market optimism, the concentration of returns in a handful of mega-cap tech stocks raises questions about sustainability.

The tech sector, once celebrated for capital efficiency, has entered a more capital-intensive phase. AI-related spending, previously funded largely by free cash flow, is increasingly fueled by debt issuance. Another notable spending trend: The biggest hyperscalers and chipmakers are funneling billions of their investment dollars into one another through circular deals that amplify sector-specific risks.

Beneath the surface, however, the story is more nuanced. For example, value-oriented stocks remain attractively priced relative to historical averages (see Figure 1), suggesting potential for mean reversion over time.

Figure 1: Value stocks may have more room to run

Macro conditions could provide a tailwind for value in the near term. An outlook for trend-like U.S. economic growth should help broaden earnings growth across sectors in 2026. In our view, the best scenario for value is if the Fed continues cutting rates into reaccelerating and broadening growth.

We also see opportunities to diversify globally. Central banks in emerging markets (EM), having established stronger monetary policy frameworks, now have more flexibility to ease policy and stimulate domestic demand, potentially supporting EM equities. Specifically, we see attractive opportunities in Korea and Taiwan, which offer exposure to the tech sector at cheaper valuations, and China.

Investor takeaway: Focus on value and quality

With high valuations concentrated among a small number of companies, it’s not difficult to find attractively valued stocks with desirable characteristics such as robust balance sheets and healthy growth. Consider tilting toward undervalued sectors rather than chasing the most expensive parts of the market.

Cash is not a strategy: The case for fixed income

In our view, investors who continue holding excess cash today are missing a potential opportunity.

Unusually high returns attracted investors to cash during the post-pandemic period of elevated inflation and Fed interest rate hikes (see Figure 2). But the current Fed rate-cutting phase brings opportunity costs and reinvestment risk, as cash holdings are repeatedly rolled into lower-yielding instruments.

Figure 2: Money market fund assets have kept climbing even as cash rates have declined

As yield curves have steepened, cash yields have declined relative to a variety of bond maturities. Bonds allow investors the potential to lock in income at more favorable levels over longer horizons.

When interest rates fall, cash earns less, but bonds typically gain in value, enhancing total return potential. At today’s yields, high quality bonds look attractive across many possible economic scenarios. With inflation having moved back toward central bank targets, bonds again provide opportunities for diversification through their traditional negative correlation to stocks, helping portfolios weather equity downturns.

Investors can also take advantage of today’s abundance of global fixed income opportunities, with attractive real and nominal yields available in countries across developed and emerging markets, such as the U.K., Australia, Peru, and South Africa (for more, see PIMCO’s October 2025 Cyclical Outlook, “Tariffs, Technology, and Transition”). We believe diversification across regions and currencies is an effective way to harvest differentiated sources of return while fortifying portfolios.

Investor takeaway: Seek to lock in yields with bonds

Consider rotating from cash into high-quality bonds for the potential to lock in yields and position for capital appreciation as interest rates decline. We favor 2- to 5-year bond maturities.

All that glitters: Gold, crypto, and the search for real assets

Gold’s extraordinary rally – recently topping $4,300/oz – has captured widespread attention. Prices have soared to all-time highs even in a generally risk-on market environment. Investor demand for inflation protection, geopolitical hedging, and diversification away from the U.S. dollar has reinforced gold’s role as a strategic asset. Central banks now hold more gold than U.S. Treasuries (see Figure 3), reflecting a shift in reserve management.

Figure 3: Foreign central banks hold more gold than Treasuries

The geopolitical backdrop remains a key driver. The 2022 seizure of Russian reserves helped catalyze gold accumulation as a politically neutral store of value. This trend, coupled with persistent trade frictions and rising sovereign debt, suggests structural support for gold demand. A potential gold price increase of more than 10% over the next year is feasible, in our view.

However, gold’s recent rally has been fueled by momentum and liquidity as much as by fundamentals, and short-term retracements are possible. While falling interest rates reduce the opportunity cost of holding gold, its valuation appears elevated relative to real yields, warranting careful sizing within portfolios.

Since 2020, commodity indices have delivered returns comparable to global equities but with lower volatility,1 reinforcing their role as diversifiers and inflation hedges. Historical evidence shows that even modest allocations to commodities can improve portfolio efficiency, particularly when inflation runs slightly above central bank targets.

Broad commodities can also provide a potential alternative way to play the AI investment theme, as infrastructure needs drive demand for inputs such as copper, lithium, and energy as well as strategic assets like rare earths.

Crypto assets, led by bitcoin, continue to evolve as digital analogs to gold, appealing to younger investors and those concerned about currency debasement. The recent decline in bitcoin reminds investors that it is a volatile instrument and perhaps not a true store of value. The rise of stablecoins and tokenized assets points to a transformative year ahead for digital finance, though volatility, tax treatment, and regulatory uncertainty remain significant considerations.

Investor takeaway: Enhance diversification and inflation protection

Consider modest, diversified allocations across gold and broad commodities for the potential to enhance portfolio resilience and inflation protection without overconcentrating in any single theme.

Credit markets: Risks and rewards along the credit continuum

Credit spreads remain tight. PIMCO has cautioned throughout 2025 about risks in certain lower-rated credit sectors, particularly within private market areas that have experienced sharp growth. We are now seeing some of those challenges surface, including recent bankruptcies and instances of fraud, which may be symptomatic of broader late-cycle laxity in credit underwriting.

Challenges are showing up in other ways. On average, shares of publicly traded business development companies (BDCs), investment vehicles for corporate direct lending, are trading at roughly a 10% discount to their net asset values. This suggests that the market is cautious about a combination of declining dividends (due to falling short interest rates) and rising credit problems. Indeed, against a strong equity market backdrop, we have also seen share price declines this year for major alternatives firms.

While debt service may become easier with lower interest rates, we have observed more privately financed companies seeking amendments to their loans or paying their debt with additional debt – known as payment-in-kind (PIK) financing – both of which can be signs of debt-servicing challenges (see Figure 4). In fact, using PIK and other data, Lincoln International calculates a “shadow default rate” of 6% as of August 2025, up from 2% in 2021.

Figure 4: Payment-in-kind (PIK) trends can signal debt-servicing challenges

Amid these strains in certain areas, PIMCO sees ongoing opportunities in credit markets for investors who can look beyond whether an investment is public or private (for more, see our latest View From the Investment Committee video, “Starting Valuations Fuel 2025 Bond Performance, 2026 Potential”). In our view, the key is to focus on evaluating liquidity and credit risk across both areas and finding where the potential rewards are greatest.

We are seeing opportunities in certain large-scale financings, where competition is limited; in credit linked to lower-risk consumers; and in select real estate lending. Longer-term trends – such as the buildup of home equity among more affluent borrowers and the expansion of AI and related energy needs – create potential opportunities to provide high quality financing with returns that can rival or exceed those of lower-quality, leveraged corporate credit.

For example, while we have a cautious outlook in general for data centers and AI, many hyperscalers are strong, investment-grade companies that need to invest – and borrow – to build out AI infrastructure. We have seen compelling opportunities in recent months in project finance, or lending secured by data centers that are being built with leases in place to investment-grade tenants. Such financings do not necessarily reflect a sector-level call but rather represent opportunities with high barriers to entry, which can result in attractive valuations, structures, and tenants.

Investor takeaway: Consider active, flexible credit strategies

2026 will likely shed light on whether recent challenges are indicative of broader underwriting problems in lower-rated credit. In the meantime, we would suggest investors think carefully about the positions they hold and whether they are being compensated for their potential credit risk, illiquidity, declining yields, and lack of transparency. The credit continuum offers many opportunities to finely tune exposure to each of these risks, and investors should use the full toolkit. At a time when many direct-lending approaches resemble passive “buy the market” strategies, we believe investors should consider being active in their credit allocations. Consider flexible strategies that are agnostic between public and private in their pursuit of superior risk- and liquidity-adjusted returns.

Conclusion

Across asset classes, a common thread is the need for active decision-making in 2026. Dispersion in equity returns, shifting interest rate dynamics, and the evolving interplay of public and private credit markets underscore the importance of independent investment research and risk management. Rather than chasing crowded trades or relying on static allocations, investors should consider strategies that balance liquidity, return potential, and diversification, while remaining flexible enough to seize new opportunities as they emerge.

Ultimately, 2026 may reward investors who embrace today’s macroeconomic environment: leaning into high quality fixed income as rates decline, selectively adding real assets for resilience amid geopolitical and inflation risks, and identifying undervalued equity sectors amid a concentrated market. In a world where uncertainty persists alongside optimism, thoughtful portfolio construction will be key.

Footnotes

1 Source: Bloomberg and PIMCO data from 31 December 2019 through 30 September 2025. Asset class proxy indices are as follows: commodities – Bloomberg Commodity Index Total Return; gold – Bloomberg Gold Total Return Index; global equities – MSCI World Index – Global Equities.

Disclosures

Past performance is not a guarantee or a reliable indicator of future results.

All investments contain risk and may lose value. Investing in the bond market is subject to risks, including market, interest rate, issuer, credit, inflation risk, and liquidity risk. The value of most bonds and bond strategies are impacted by changes in interest rates. Bonds and bond strategies with longer durations tend to be more sensitive and volatile than those with shorter durations; bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise, and low interest rate environments increase this risk. Reductions in bond counterparty capacity may contribute to decreased market liquidity and increased price volatility. Bond investments may be worth more or less than the original cost when redeemed. Private credit involves an investment in non-publicly traded securities which may be subject to illiquidity risk. Portfolios that invest in private credit may be leveraged and may engage in speculative investment practices that increase the risk of investment loss. Equities may decline in value due to both real and perceived general market, economic and industry conditions. Diversification does not ensure against loss. Commodities contain heightened risk, including market, political, regulatory and natural conditions, and may not be appropriate for all investors. The value of real estate and portfolios that invest in real estate may fluctuate due to: losses from casualty or condemnation, changes in local and general economic conditions, supply and demand, interest rates, property tax rates, regulatory limitations on rents, zoning laws, and operating expenses.

Forecasts, estimates and certain information contained herein are based upon proprietary research and should not be interpreted as investment advice, as an offer or solicitation, nor as the purchase or sale of any financial instrument. Forecasts and estimates have certain inherent limitations, and unlike an actual performance record, do not reflect actual trading, liquidity constraints, fees, and/or other costs. In addition, references to future results should not be construed as an estimate or promise of results that a client portfolio may achieve.

Forecasts, estimates and certain information contained herein are based upon proprietary research and should not be interpreted as investment advice, as an offer or solicitation, nor as the purchase or sale of any financial instrument. Forecasts and estimates have certain inherent limitations, and unlike an actual performance record, do not reflect actual trading, liquidity constraints, fees, and/or other costs. In addition, references to future results should not be construed as an estimate or promise of results that a client portfolio may achieve.

Statements concerning financial market trends or portfolio strategies are based on current market conditions, which will fluctuate. There is no guarantee that these investment strategies will work under all market conditions or are appropriate for all investors and each investor should evaluate their ability to invest for the long term, especially during periods of downturn in the market. Investors should consult their investment professional prior to making an investment decision. Outlook and strategies are subject to change without notice.

Correlation is a statistical measure of how two securities move in relation to each other. Duration is the measure of a bond's price sensitivity to interest rates and is expressed in years.

PIMCO does not provide legal or tax advice.

PIMCO as a general matter provides services to qualified institutions, financial intermediaries and institutional investors. Individual investors should contact their own financial professional to determine the most appropriate investment options for their financial situation. This material contains the opinions of the manager and such opinions are subject to change without notice. This material has been distributed for informational purposes only and should not be considered as investment advice or a recommendation of any particular security, strategy or investment product. Information contained herein has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but not guaranteed. No part of this material may be reproduced in any form, or referred to in any other publication, without express written permission. PIMCO is a trademark of Allianz Asset Management of America LLC in the United States and throughout the world.

CMR2025-1121-5014008

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Panasonic: Investor Day Disclosures And Housing Business Sale Draw Attention
data.description:
I'm still bullish about Panasonic Holdings (OTCPK:PCRFF) (OTCPK:PCRHY).

I've evaluated its Investor Day disclosures and latest corporate transaction. My take is that PCRFF's outlook is favorable.

The previous December 11, 2024 article discussed my optimistic view of its GenAI- and HVAC-related units.

Investor Event Casts Spotlight On BBU Operations

PCRFF recently hosted an "Investor Relations/IR" Day on Dec 2. The transcript was sourced from S&P Capital IQ.

Management's presentation was focused on its "Battery Backup Unit/BBU" division serving the "Data Center/DC" end-market. This is anticipated to be one of the group's fastest growing and most profitable businesses.

The firm has projected that BBU's turnover will quadruple from slightly below ¥0.2B for FY25 (YE Mar 31, 2025) to more than ¥0.8B in FY29. PCRFF's actual FY2021-2025 revenue CAGR was +6%.

The positive expectations regarding the battery segment are realistic in my opinion.

Around four-fifths of the forecasted BBU revenue for FY2029 is covered by existing "awards." That is defined as "projects agreed upon with customers to advance product development and secure orders for the developed products" by PCRFF.

Also, there is a robust launch pipeline. The company anticipates that 20% of its FY29 BBU-related sales will be derived from "next-generation products" like those used for "power supply racks."

It's important to note that PCRFF is a leader in this huge market. Gartner estimates that the global server industry will expand at an annualized rate of +34% during the 2023-2028 timeframe to $0.22T. The group has a 80% share of DC BBUs according to its own research. These numbers were obtained from its IR Day disclosures.

Separately, PCRFF has described BBU as a "double-digit margin" business at its 2QFY26 analyst briefing on Oct 30 this year. Its group-level EBITDA-to-sales and net margin were 9.4% and 4.0%, respectively on a trailing twelve months' basis.

I think that the battery operations can sustain high profitability.

PCRFF's end-Oct results call commentary emphasized that BBU is "not such an asset-heavy business." This division's capital intensity is at a moderate level, which translates into decent margins.

At the Investor Day, it indicated that the "growth is not just driven by the sales of the (battery) cell as a stand-alone product." Integrated solutions can command a price premium.

PCRFF has also been reconfiguring its existing automotive battery plants to support the increase in DC-focused BBU capacity. That implies a lower amount of capital expenditures is needed to drive future growth.

Housing Subsidiary Divestiture Signals Corporate Restructuring Progress

Last month, PCRFF issued an announcement revealing the move to "transfer 80% of the shares" of "Panasonic Housing Solutions, Co., Ltd. (“PHS”), a wholly-owned" entity to a third party called "YKK Corporation."

I see the above-mentioned as a value-accretive deal.

The divestment was done at an implied EV/EBITDA of 9x, considering PHS's "Enterprise Value/EV" of ¥228B. This is better than PCRFF's forward EV/EBITDA of 6 times (source: S&P Capital IQ).

Its FY2026 operating income is expected to receive a +¥60B boost from the latest transaction. That represented 18% of the consensus's full-year forecast for the company as a whole.

PCRFF's net cash will also rise by ¥0.1T, thanks to PHS's stake reduction.

The Firm's Segments Targeted For Reorganization

As per the chart presented above, "Housing" is among the "low-profit businesses" that it has been planning to deal with.

It's encouraging to learn that PCRFF has taken a big step towards realizing financial improvement by selling the majority of PHS shares.

Earlier, the group has also been successful in turning around some of the other units.

At the 2QFY2026 earnings briefing, PCRFF noted that "we can move the TV business out of the (category of) businesses with issues", and mentioned "the same goes for kitchen appliances."

It has cut the kitchen appliances operations' costs substantially by shifting most of the sourcing and manufacturing to China. Also, the television division benefited from "collaboration with partners" and becoming "as lean as possible" as per the Q2 management call remarks.

Looking ahead, there are opportunities for PCRFF to overhaul the others such as "Industrial Devices", "Electromechanical Control", "Consumer Electronics", and "Air-conditioning."

My prior Dec '24 update touched on how the "HVAC business' future performance" can be enhanced by focusing on "the comparatively higher-margin operations in Europe and Asia (excluding Japan)" and driving down "the proportion of fixed costs."

The sell-side is projecting that PCRFF's EBITDA-to-revenue can go from FY25's 9.8% to 12.6% and 13.5% in FY27 and FY28, respectively as per S&P Capital IQ. This is achievable, taking into account the ongoing business reforms.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Variant Views

A slower-than-expected pace of DC investments could hurt demand for the company's BBU-related products.

PCRFF's actual margin expansion might disappoint investors. That can happen if it executes poorly on restructuring initiatives.

My Closing Remarks

I've maintained a 'Buy' rating for PCRFF.

I value the stock with the "Gordon Growth Model." My assumptions are a 10% ROE (company's goal), a 9% Cost of Equity (market average), and a +3% Perpetuity Growth Rate (developed economy's expansion rate).

That gives me a target "Price-to-Book" or "P/B" of 1.17 times.

Its current P/B is 0.91x, so the shares can possibly go up by +29% (1.17/0.91).

PCRFF has both growth and profitability levers to justify a re-rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Warning To Young Dividend Investors
data.description:
If you've been a follower of mine, you'll know that I've almost exclusively covered dividend-paying stocks and funds. This includes everything from the traditional blue-chip dividend stocks to the alternative asset classes such as business development companies, REITs, closed-end funds, and now option ETFs. With the growing demand amongst investors for income generation, we've seen a rise in the inflows of capital towards these investment products. While I completely understand the appeal of these funds, I feel an obligation to issue a warning to younger dividend investors that may be making some of the same mistakes that I've lived through.

I was lucky enough to get my first big boy job that had me in a cubicle office at 19 years old. I worked full time and went to school at nights. Since I had an office job a lot earlier than some of my peers, I learned quickly that the corporate world wasn't something that I enjoyed. So I started early with the concept of investing into dividend stocks that can eventually generate enough income to replace a salary. I stayed committed to this process of building a dividend for several years and I eventually worked my way up to a point where my dividend income now pays more than the salary I received at that first job.

The idea was that I wanted to build an income stream that could pay all my expenses, so that I have the opportunity to pursue whatever I wanted. However, the entire way I viewed investing had been wrong. I took a very static approach of focusing on dividend income, despite my life priorities, values, and challenges constantly shifting over the decade. I got older and I eventually figured out what I enjoyed, expenses and life obligations increased, and I no longer had a desire to retire early with dividends.

So as we approach the new year, I have started to reflect back on some of the mistakes I made. I feel that I have an obligation to bring some of these issues to light in an attempt to help some of you younger investors avoid the same mistakes that I made.

Dividends Have Limitations

The beginning premise of building my dividend portfolio was quite simple. I wanted to build a foundation using stocks that I directly consumed every single month. I scrolled social media every single day on my iPhone, so I bought Apple (AAPL). I washed my clothes, brushed my teeth, and cleaned my house using products from Procter & Gamble (PG), so I added that. I'm not a drinker but I loved having the occasional soda, so Coca-Cola (KO) deserved a spot in the portfolio. You get the idea!

Eventually, I accumulated over 25 different positions in dividend companies that consistently produced income for me every month. The only problem was that I was a young guy who wasn't making a lot of money at the time. So my capacity to add capital into these stocks were limited and the process of building this portfolio took a lot longer than I would have liked.

While my dividend portfolio did experience some nice growth over the years, the biggest downfall was that the focus on dividends caused me to under allocate to technology stocks. As you may already know, there are much fewer technology stocks that pay out dividends. As a result, I automatically dismissed many of the companies that aligned with the concept of investing in the things I consume. For instance, here were some of the stocks I dismissed and their respective share prices ten years ago, which didn't pay out dividends a decade ago:

*These numbers are post-splits, going back to 2015 levels

  • Netflix (NFLX): ~$11.89 per share
  • Amazon (AMZN): ~$33.21 per share
  • Alphabet (GOOG): ~$36.94 per share
  • Meta Platforms (META): ~$102.12 per share

The end result is that my portfolio substantially underperformed traditional indexes, such as the S&P 500 and Nasdaq-100. While I eventually snapped out of it and added some of these companies, I already missed hundreds of percentage points worth of growth. I failed to realize that if I would've simply remained exposed to technology through a straightforward ETF, such as the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), I would've had a lot more money at the end of the road. If I really wanted to still focus on dividends, I could have always sold portions of the gains from these holdings and allocated them to dividend stocks.

While we can always argue that tech's past performance is no indication of the future returns, I think that would be a mistake. Precedence Research estimates that the size of the AI market is expected to increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 19.20% through 2034. As companies continue to allocate hundreds of billions towards AI infrastructure, it's clear which direction the world is heading. For instance, META confirmed plans to allocate $600B in capex to build out the AI infrastructure through data centers.

Growth Makes Wealth Building Easier

Don't mistake what I am saying! Dividends are still an integral aspect for many investors and I am not oblivious to the use case of them. However, I specifically want younger investors to know that there's a certain tradeoff you are choosing, and perhaps you don't even realize it.

There is a growing popularity in covered call ETFs that still track an underlying index. Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) is one of the most popular examples because you are still getting exposure to constituents of the Nasdaq-100, while also collecting a dividend yield of 9.6%. The issue is that GPIQ's options limit the growth potential of the fund, as I highlight in my recent article. It needs to be understood that a high dividend yield, does not automatically equal a high total return.

Since my portfolio grew at a slower pace than the rest of the market indices, I did a lot of the heavy lifting in order to fuel my growth. I took on new jobs across different industries and put myself through a lot of overtime work in order to make more money. I did this with the sole intention of investing more capital over time.

However, it would've been a lot easier on my body and mind if my portfolio was capturing a greater level of growth, so that I didn't need to do all the heavy lifting on my own. So without even realizing it, I was working myself harder than I probably needed to and I could've eased off the gas a lot sooner if my portfolio was better constructed with growth.

The irony of it all was that I wasn't spending any of the dividends I received anyways. I genuinely took an interest in seeing my wealth grow over time so I always reinvested those dollars. So if I wasn't spending the dividends, we could've made the argument that focusing on growth would have been more beneficial. By focusing on dividends, I was holding a lot of companies that weren't innovating on their products or services, and they were companies that already passed their prime growth spur.

Unexpected Impacts Of Yield Chasing

When I first started including the alternative asset classes, I became obsessed with the high yields that I could receive. REITs like Realty Income (O) offered a starting dividend yield above 5%, while business development companies like Ares Capital (ARCC) offered a dividend yield around 10%. As a result, I had a phase where I over allocated my cash towards these higher yielding positions, which created minimal price growth and the unexpected tax burden.

While I find the concept of these investments really cool, I think the utility is a lot more specific for each investor. If you buy these sort of high yield positions within a regular taxable brokerage account, you will be faced with ordinary dividends, which are taxed at the highest rate for investors. Based on the amount of dividend income you collect from these type of securities, you may be faced with an unexpected tax bill that is much larger than you'd like to pay at the end of the year.

Yield chasing also led me to include assets like MLPs (Master Limited Partnerships), such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD). This taught me how much extra work filing my taxes can be, when I need to wait until late March to receive a K-1 form. MLPs can be structured as a pass-through entity, which provides special tax benefits. However, the small income I was receiving from these entities didn't outweigh the burden I faced during tax time.

Takeaway

While I still think dividends are a great aspect to the wealth building experience, I want young investors to avoid a lot of the same mistakes that I made. Sometimes it makes more sense to focus on growth positions that can exponentially increase your wealth faster than you can add new capital over time. By focusing only on dividend-paying companies, you may be aligning your portfolio to underperform traditional indices over time. While the income generated within your portfolio may be giving you a sense of security, it may be coming at the cost of having less money at the end of the road. Yield chasing into higher dividend yield positions can also have some unexpected impacts besides stunted growth, such as a high tax bill and unnecessary paperwork during tax time.

data.title: NextEra: Strong Regulatory Backdrop And Renewables Leadership Support A Higher Target Price
data.description:
After our recent review of the major EU utilities, including Enel and Engie, we are now turning to an update on NextEra (NEE). It has been some time since our last update. Here at the Lab, we were among the few to highlight the link between datacenter expansion and rising power demand (Fig. 2). Yet, the share price and dividends have only delivered a low double-digit return so far (Fig. 1). Given its clear edge in US renewables and its still-compelling valuation, especially when compared with the recent re-rating of EU utilities, we believe the company is well positioned to beat the sector over the coming 12 months. This view is reinforced by solid Q3 results and, more importantly, the new FPL rate agreement.

Fig 1

Source: NextEra Q3 results ppt - Fig 2

Why are we still positive?

We will first touch briefly on the Q3 results, then move on to the latest agreement and our upside case.

The company reported a Q3 2025 adjusted earnings of $1.13 per share compared to a $1.03 result in the prior year quarter (and above the expectation of $1.06) (Fig. 3). Florida Power and Light (FPL) earnings improved to $1.4 billion with an EPS of $0.71. Strong investment activities supported this. On a positive note, NextEra Energy Resources also reported earnings of $1.27 billion and EPS of $0.62. Compared to last quarter, FPL's EPS improved thanks to new investments and higher customer growth (+1.5%), offset by lower usage due to weather and modestly higher usage from a change in retail sales. Adjusted earnings at NextEra Energy Resources were helped by higher customer supply and offset by asset recycling and higher financing costs.

Fig 3

The Florida PSC’s approval of the rate case settlement provides FPL with a very supportive regulatory backdrop, and we see scope for EPS to compound in the high single digits (8%+). Structural demand from data centres should underpin the regulated outlook in Florida and drive further investment in generation and transmission across the US. In our view, a firmer power-price environment helps cushion recontracting risk, while opportunities in NextEra's battery storage, solar, and gas businesses are set to expand.

The commission approved the four-year rate settlement as filed. Key features include:

  • Return framework: an allowed ROE band of 9.95–11.95% with a 59.6% equity ratio.

  • Stabilisation tools: use of around $1.15 billion of deferred tax liabilities and ITCs tied to 522 MW of storage entering service in 2025.

  • Large-load tariff: a dedicated large-customer tariff with a 20-year term and a two-year termination notice.

  • Customer impact: the deal implies roughly a 2% average annual bill increase through 2029, with typical FPL residential bills still about 20% below levels of 20 years ago after inflation adjustment.

  • Rate increases: base-rate hikes of $945 million in 2026 and $705 million in 2027, plus a SOBRA (solar and battery rate adjustment) mechanism for solar (2027–2029) and storage (2028–2029) additions.

Management already previewed these themes at the Edison Electric conference, and we expect the 8 December investor day to elaborate further.

At NextEra, the development pipeline is the largest in the company’s history, with about 30 GW of backlog benefiting from FEOC protection and safe-harbor status, skewed towards solar and storage (Fig. 4). In Q3, the company also added another 3 GW of renewables and storage to this backlog. We think investor expectations for gas generation and infrastructure remain conservative, and NextEra is likely to highlight incremental opportunities in these areas.

Fig 4

The planned restart of the Duane Arnold nuclear plant is expected to add roughly $0.16 EPS on average over the first ten years from 2029 (Fig. 5), and we look for an updated assessment of the upside from expiring PPAs as contracts roll off.

Fig 5

Regarding NextEra Energy Transmission, we expect the company to focus on opportunities in Texas, SPP, and MISO, and there may also be commentary on FERC’s proposed large-load interconnection rule. On funding, as tax credits roll off, the company will need to spell out its capital plan, potentially combining asset recycling, private credit structures, and hybrid/equity-linked instruments.

Earnings Changes and Valuation

The company reaffirmed its EPS guidance for 2025-2026-2027, with yearly midpoints of $3.58, $3.82, and $4.08 (Fig. 6). This implied EPS growth near the high end of the 6-8% range. We see positive developments with Google regarding the restart of the Duane Arnold nuclear facility, and following the Florida PSC’s approval, we believe the company is also de-risked. Finally, we would expect the company to address its backlog of contracts serving technology and data center customers. Continuing to apply a 25x P/E multiple (Fig. 7) to the 2026 EPS of $3.82, we reached a valuation of $95.5 per share (vs our last valuation of $89 per share). Therefore, we continue to buy the company.

Fig 6

Fig 7

Risk

NextEra's downside risks include higher interest rates due to its debt, regulatory changes, and policy risks. The company has a 30 GW backlog, and we recognized the risks of CAPEX inflation, procurement, and poor project execution. There are also risks related to nuclear operations and the gas pipeline. The company is subject to cybersecurity attacks in the transmission grid and extreme weather conditions.

Conclusion

The company is still well placed to capture incremental power demand from AI/data centres, onshoring, and electrification. At the same time, exposure to wind and solar tax credits appears manageable. On the back of the favorable Florida rate decision, we have reiterated our Buy recommendation and lifted our target price.

data.title: CareTrust: The REIT That's Outperforming The S&P 500
data.description:
Introduction

On my investment journey, I have come across different styles of investors. From income-focused ones like myself to growth-focused and high-yield investors, everyone's strategy should be tailored to their individual needs.

For me, being income-focused, REITs (XLRE) have played an integral part in my portfolio-building process, thanks to their higher yields.

And while many investors are turned off by the sector, particularly over the past 3 years, some REITs have performed exceptionally. And this is despite the negative sentiment surrounding them.

Due to their interest rate sensitivity and lower growth, many have lost their appeal since interest rates skyrocketed starting in 2022. However, CareTrust REIT, Inc. (CTRE) has not only continued to perform, it has outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500).

In this article, I discuss CareTrust REIT's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why, despite their strong performance, this REIT could continue outperforming over the longer term.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered CTRE this past August in an article titled: One Of My Highest Conviction REITs Has Crushed The Market, And I'm Still Bullish.

Since then, CareTrust REIT has continued to outperform, up nearly 11% to 6.27% for the index at the time of writing. CTRE's performance has been driven by their strong growth, a result of their higher investment activity over the past two years.

Debunking the Underperformance Myth

Most of us have heard the story of REITs being underperformers. And while a higher-for-longer interest rate environment does negatively impact REITs' cost of capital, CTRE has been the exception.

As you can see, I've been highly bullish on CTRE dating back to my first article written on them in September of 2023. Since then, the stock is up nearly 86% in comparison to roughly 53% for the S&P.

So, although some REITs have underperformed amidst rising interest rates, CareTrust REIT has debunked the myth of REITs. And with most likely to see tailwinds from lower base rates over the next 12 to 24 months, CTRE looks poised for more upside.

CTRE's performance can be attributed to their strong investments over the past two years. Their performance should be commended considering the tough economic backdrop, which has resulted in slower investment activity for many.

Year-to-date, investments have exceeded the $1.5 billion in acquisitions in 2024. Their latest acquisition saw the REIT continue to expand internationally with the purchase of two care homes for $27 million. The U.K. now accounts for 15.1% of annualized base rent, second to California at 19.5%.

The acquisition was made with cash, showcasing the strength of the company's balance sheet. At the end of Q3, their net debt to EBITDA was a very low 0.4x, one of the lowest leverages I've seen amongst any REIT. Most healthcare REITs operate in the high 4x to high 5x range.

This, and their well-staggered debt, gives the company ample room to see increased investment activity. The REIT's first debt maturity isn't until 2028, with $400 million due, and the next maturity isn't until 2030. Liquidity was strong with more than $300 million in cash and $1.2 billion in revolver capacity.

And I think going into 2026, we could see acquisitions even exceed those in 2024 and 2025. If so, this will likely continue to drive price appreciation over the long term.

Although healthcare stocks typically perform well during times of uncertainty, CTRE's long-term outperformance is something I think investors highly overlook.

For comparison, I put CareTrust up against the most popular and largest S&P ETF, Vanguard's (VOO). And you can see that over the past 1, 3, 5, and even 10 years, the REIT has significantly outperformed the ETF.

1-year: 25.93% vs. 13.15%

3 years: 100.16% vs. 72.12%

5 years: 93.77% vs. 88.34%

10 years: 255.22% vs. 225.22%

10-year total return: 476.03% vs. 286.51%

Although CTRE's yield isn't as attractive as other high-quality REITs like Realty Income's (O) 5.62% or Agree Realty's (ADC) 4.08%, there's still a respectable 3.46%.

Additionally, REITs that have lower yields have seen stronger price appreciation, which improves their cost of capital. This also enables them to issue shares well above their NAVs to raise capital for future growth.

During their latest quarter in early November, CTRE's results were mixed, with a miss on FFO by $0.02. Revenue of $132.44 million beat by roughly $10 million and grew a staggering 71% from $77.4 million the year prior.

More importantly, CTRE grew every metric during the quarter, as evidenced by the chart below. And they ended Q3 with a healthy payout ratio of 74%.

FAD, or funds available for distribution, similar to AFFO, grew from $0.37 to $0.45 on a per-share basis. In total, growth was strong, up roughly 51% to $95.1 million. As far as investments, CTRE made $59 million in acquisitions and $437 million subsequent to the quarter's end. Their pipeline was also strong at $600 million.

Significant Upside May Be Limited

Using management's guidance midpoint of $1.765, this gives CTRE a forward multiple of 21.26x at the current price. Although I remain bullish on the REIT for the long term, I suggest investors wait for any signs of share price weakness, preferably around $32 or below.

At the current price, CareTrust REIT appears overvalued, as they sit well above their normal multiple of 14.27x. However, due to their strong performance and higher growth, I think it's fair to assume they'll likely trade anywhere between 18x and 20x going forward and are currently fairly valued.

Wall Street remains bullish on the stock with a price target near $40 a share. Still, this gives investors little margin of safety, as the current share price sits closer to $38 a share.

Risks

I think CTRE's biggest risk is they could see potential downside or lower returns due to their strong price appreciation. Going forward, price appreciation could be very limited, suggesting investors should wait for a pullback closer to the $30 level.

Additionally, the recent uptick in unemployment could be an indication of a slower 2026 despite the economy appearing to remain resilient.

As a result, I think the chances of a recession are higher than many are leading us to believe. If so, acquisition activity could slow dramatically in the coming years, leading to lower growth.

Bottom Line

Although I remain cautiously bullish on CareTrust REIT shares due to potential tailwinds from lower base rates, macro uncertainty remains a risk going forward.

If we do manage to see a recession, I suspect investment activity could slow, leading to lower growth and less upside.

And while I expect the REIT to navigate, their chances of beating the S&P going forward are lower since CTRE appears fairly valued here. For investors looking to buy, I think you could get a margin of safety near the $30 level.

data.title: BITW: Expensive Fund With Near Perfect Correlation To Bitcoin
data.description:
The Bitwise 10 Crypto Index Fund (OTC:BITW) is a diversified cryptocurrency investment strategy designed to provide investors with exposure to the top 10 cryptocurrencies by market capitalization. BITW can potentially be utilized by investors seeking a diversified portfolio of cryptocurrencies; however, the vast majority of assets are allocated to bitcoin, resulting in near-perfect correlation between BITW and bitcoin. Given the relative underperformance when compared to the historical performance of bitcoin and the challenging market dynamics in the cryptocurrency market, I am recommending BITW with a Sell rating.

About Bitwise 10 Crypto Index Fund

BITW was launched on November 22, 2017 as a Trust with Bitwise Investment Advisers as the Sponsor of the Trust. The Trust was later brought to the public markets on December 9, 2020 on the OTCQX Exchange. The Trust was converted to an exchange-traded product following SEC approval on November 18, 2025 and is now listed on the NYSE Arca Exchange.

BITW was designed to track the Bitwise 10 Large Cap Crypto Index, which is administered by Bitwise Index Services. The Index was designed to track the top 10 largest cryptocurrencies by market capitalization, which captures an estimated 75% of the total market capitalization of the cryptocurrency market. The Index is reconstituted monthly, and in turn, the ETF is rebalanced on a monthly basis.

BITW has a net expense ratio of 250bps, a relatively high fee when compared to peer cryptocurrency portfolio strategies. Despite offering a diversified cryptocurrency portfolio, investors must weigh whether the differentiated returns over bitcoin provide enough value to justify the relatively high management fee.

Looking through the portfolio, BITW is largely concentrated amongst the top holdings with bitcoin weighted at 74.7% and Ethereum at 14.8%. Given that portfolio weightings are dictated by market capitalization, smaller large-cap coins may be less represented in the portfolio, and thus, less influential to the value of the fund.

Looking at the performance of the portfolio, many of the smaller coins have significantly underperformed bitcoin in 2025 with bitcoin returning -6% year-to-date. In addition to this, BITW has substantially underperformed bitcoin across all historical holding periods, raising the question as to whether an investor needs to diversify across crypto assets or whether a simple investment in bitcoin will suffice.

The cryptocurrency has experienced a cascading effect in recent months, potentially driven by the imbalance between fund inflows & outflows. This is a factor that may influence the price and flows of bitcoin, particularly as more retirement accounts include an allocation to bitcoin funds. Prior to the approval of bitcoin spot ETFs in January 2024, bitcoin had a limited investor pool given the necessity for an additional brokerage account or limited options for investment vehicles. A major change that came with the emergence of bitcoin spot ETFs was the addition of advisory operations, which includes portfolio rebalances, cash raises, and charitable giving, amongst other features, adding risks that had previously not impacted bitcoin. I believe bitcoin may be exceptionally exposed given the liquidity and trading depth of the underlying asset, allowing for an appealing vehicle for capital raises.

Another driving factor in the cryptocurrency market is the use of leveraged funds. Accordingly, over $1t in market capitalization has been wiped out since October 2025 with the price of bitcoin falling by nearly -35% from its high in October 2025 before recovering some ground. Much of the price decline was driven by investors unwinding leveraged bitcoin strategies, which are estimated to be in the range of $19-30b on October 10, 2025.

Though I believe a small 1-2% allocation to cryptocurrency is appropriate for a diversified investment portfolio, investors must consider the amount of risk they are willing to take on by diversifying into smaller tokens held in BITW, such as Polkadot (DOT-USD) or Avalanche (AVAX-USD). Given the relative performance of the diversified portfolio when compared to bitcoin, investors must weight whether diversifying into riskier assets is appropriate. Overall, I believe investing in a bitcoin spot ETF like the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT) will provide appropriate coverage in the cryptocurrency market, at least for the average investor. Given the additional risks, volatility, and relatively smaller positions of alternative coins, BITW may expose investors to more market risk than is necessary for cryptocurrency exposure.

Risks Related to BITW

BITW provides investors with a diversified portfolio strategy for investing in alternative coins, presenting certain risks that must be considered prior to making a final investment decision. Smaller cryptocurrencies may expose investors to significant volatility that may add unwanted risk to one’s portfolio. BITW is heavily concentrated in bitcoin and Ethereum and is heavily correlated with bitcoin at 97%, potentially exposing investors to greater risk and a higher management fee without a substantial amount of value being derived from the alternative coins held.

Final Thoughts

BITW provides investors with diversified exposure across the top 10 cryptocurrencies by market capitalization, differentiating exposure from bitcoin spot ETFs. Given the relative correlation of BITW to bitcoin, I do not believe that the ETF differentiates exposure enough to justify the high management fee, particularly given that the fund has underperformed bitcoin by a wide margin since inception. Given these factors, I am recommending BITW with a Sell rating.

data.title: Sprinklr: Large Customer Declines Are A Big Concern (Downgrade)
data.description:
As we come close to wrapping up 2025, it's becoming clear that the sustainability of 2025's sharp market rally is top of mind for many investors. To me, it's the right time to be rotating out of recent winners in the large-cap growth space and shifting more of our portfolios into beaten-down small- and mid-cap stocks. And yet in this space, individual stock-picking is incredibly important, and the companies I hold in my portfolio must both be cheap while also having upside fundamental catalysts to drive a re-rating.

Sprinklr (CXM), a customer experience management software company, is certainly cheap, but recent quarters have made me question its quality. The company just reported Q3 results that featured a beat on the top line but dragging subscription revenue amid an exodus of large customers. The stock is down modestly post-earnings, continuing a ~10% slide over the past year that has dramatically underperformed the S&P 500.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Sprinklr in September, when the stock was trading just shy of $8 per share. In my view, the fundamental outlook for Sprinklr has shifted downward: the company's subscription revenue growth has stalled, making us question whether the company has the chops to achieve meaningful operating leverage. Amid macro pressures that are constraining IT budgets on these "nice to have," but not mission critical software products, I'm downgrading Sprinklr back down to a neutral rating.

To me, the company is now a mixed bag of positives and negatives. On the bright side for the company:

  • Cheap multiples raise the possibility of a buyout. The number-one reason to buy Sprinklr is because it's cheap. Recently, lagging software companies have been the subject of M&A, both by strategic corporate acquirers as well as private equity (Adobe (ADBE), for example, just bought struggling SEO provider Semrush).
  • Clean balance sheet. Sprinklr has nearly ~$0.5 billion in cash on its balance sheet, unencumbered of debt: which represents a substantial chunk of its current market value. Its clean balance sheet, in my view, further raises Sprinklr's appeal as a potential M&A target.

At the same time, however, we're wary of the following factors:

  • Struggling with growth as the company pushes upmarket. Sprinklr's core strategy to grow is to chase higher-spending, larger enterprise customers. This has had mixed results. Net revenue retention rates are barely in expansion territory, growth is in the mid-single digits, and long-term RPO is declining.
  • Weak growth/profitability margin profile. Sprinklr's revenue growth plus its pro forma operating margins sit in the mid-20s, a far cry from the "Rule of 40" that most software companies aspire to, which is a justification for its steep discount versus peers.

We'll stress here that Sprinklr is quite cheap, especially after the year-to-date declines. At current share prices in the mid-$7s, Sprinklr trades at a market cap of $1.82 billion. Netting off the $480.4 million of cash on its most recent balance sheet, Sprinklr's resulting enterprise value is $1.34 billion.

One piece of good news is the fact that Sprinklr boosted its FY25 guidance. The company is now expecting 5% y/y subscription revenue growth (previously 4%) and 7% total revenue growth (previously 5%).

For next year, FY26, Wall Street is expecting Sprinklr to hit $874.5 million in revenue, or 4% y/y growth. This target appears conservative coming off a quarter in which Sprinklr grew total revenue at 9% y/y. But given the weaker net retention rates and low single-digit growth in current RPO, I find consensus targets to be quite reasonable. Taking this estimate at face value, Sprinklr takes at 1.5x EV/FY26 revenue.

Needless to say, this is an ultra-cheap multiple that reflects a lot of pessimism in Sprinklr's stock. We note that SEO specialist Semrush was in a similar boat prior to being taken out by Adobe at 3.6x revenue. Still, I also think Sprinklr has a healthy amount of risk baked in: declining enterprise customer counts may scare potential buyers off, and I don't see any near-term catalysts that can help to spark an upward re-rating.

All in all, I'm losing patience with this stock: it's best to lock in losses here and invest elsewhere.

Q3 download

Let's now go through Sprinklr's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Sprinklr's revenue grew 9% y/y to $219.1 million, which came in meaningfully ahead of Wall Street's weaker expectations of $209.6 million (+4% y/y). However, we note that a large rise in professional services revenue (+44% y/y) contributed to a lot of the quarter's growth. While we note this is a good indicator for new client onboardings, Sprinklr performs professional services work essentially at cost ($28.8 million of revenue, offset by $28.2 million of gross cost): its long-term scalability rests on its ability to grow its subscription base. On this front, trends have been choppier: subscription revenue of $190.3 million grew at a weaker 5% y/y pace, and decelerated a point from 6% growth in Q2.

Where we are most disappointed is that Sprinklr's upmarket sales strategy appears to be losing tailwinds. As shown in the chart below, the company now has only 145 customers who generate more than $1 million in ARR: a decline of two such customers year over year.

Another poor indicator: while the company's current RPO (backlog that will be recognized as revenue in the next 12 months) grew 3% y/y to $562.2 million, this was slower than overall subscription revenue growth. Furthermore, overall RPO (including longer-term backlog) actually fell -5% y/y to $857.6 million, and RPO balances have actually been in sequential decline every quarter for the past year:

To me, this is a signal that customers are unwilling to sign up for long-term commitments in Sprinklr. This may be a reflection of a weaker macro environment, where companies are pulling back on growth investments in sales and marketing: which impacts the kind of CX research and activities that Sprinklr promotes. Sprinklr's Employee Experience (EX) products could also be getting hit by waning corporate headcount, as companies look to streamline teams and leverage AI to perform more tasks.

Furthermore, Sprinklr's net revenue retention rates clocked in at just 102% in the quarter, which indicates only 2% net expansion: not really a ringing endorsement for Sprinklr's success at "land and expand" into larger customers who are able to purchase multiple products and onboard more teams onto the Sprinklr platform.

On the profitability front, we note that Sprinklr's pro forma operating margins still saw 4 points of y/y improvement to 15%:

Still, 5% subscription revenue growth on top of ~15% pro forma operating margins isn't exactly a cause for celebration, when even many traditional "Rule of 40" companies like HubSpot (HUBS) and Workday (WDAY) are trading down for the year and at reasonably cheap valuations in the 5-6x revenue range.

Key takeaways

Sprinklr is looking increasingly like a value trap as its subscription growth rate wanes and the company shows signs of churn among its larger enterprise customer base. Right now, there are many stocks on sale in the software sector: I'd rather pay a slightly higher multiple for a higher-quality, beaten-down stock like HubSpot or Atlassian than chase extreme value in a company like Sprinklr of more questionable quality.

data.title: Tenet Healthcare's Surge Isn't Over Yet
data.description:
One of my best calls in recent years has been Tenet Healthcare Corporation (THC). This is a fascinating business, with ownership over 50 hospitals and a growing ambulatory care business. In my latest article about the company, published in October of 2023, I found myself impressed with its growth and valuation. This led me to reaffirm it as a ‘buy’ candidate. And since then, things have gone quite well. The stock is up 288%, which is massively higher than the 58.3% rise that the S&P 500 experienced. Since my original ‘buy’ rating on it in February of the previous year, things have not gone quite as well, but they are still going great. The stock is up to 100.4% since then, which is nearly four times the 52.9% rise that the broader market enjoyed.

You would think that, after such monumental upside, it would be time for a downgrade. To be clear, I do think that there are certain risks facing the company, specifically on the regulatory side of things. And this does warrant some consideration. However, as things stand, shares look incredibly cheap, both on an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms. And with management even admitting that the regulatory exposure is not as great as it might first initially appear, I think that this still makes for a compelling ‘buy’ candidate.

Registering with Tenet Healthcare Corporation

Whenever I go a while without analyzing a company, I'd like to go back to the foundation and lay out exactly what the company does and how it operates. The way I view it, if I haven't looked at the business in a while, other investors might not have either. Fundamentally speaking, this is a fascinating business. Management describes it as a diversified healthcare services firm that's based out of Texas. It also has operations in the Philippines, but overwhelmingly, this is a US based firm.

It has, at this moment, two different operating segments. The first and largest of these is its Hospital Operations segment, which owns 50 acute care and specialty hospitals. It boasts a network of employed positions, over 100 outpatient facilities that include urgent care centers, imaging centers, off campus hospital emergency departments, and even what it calls micro hospitals. In essence, this part of the company generates revenue by providing various health care services to customers. Examples here include checkups, surgeries, consultations, and the like.

Even more impressive, to me, is the smaller Ambulatory Care division. This part of the company, which management often refers to as USPI, holds indirect ownership in hundreds of interests and 530 ambulatory surgery centers. 398 of these are consolidated, with the remaining falling under joint ventures or minority interests. He also has 26 surgical hospitals here, with eight of those being consolidated. This is a national company, with a presence in 37 states, which is far greater than the eight states that its Hospital Operations segment has a presence in. Through this unit, it offers up various procedures and services such as those involving orthopedics, total joint replacement services, spinal and other musculoskeletal procedures, gastroenterology services, pain management services, urology services, and the like.

Over the last few years now, Tenet Healthcare Corporation has seen some consistent growth on the top line. Revenue inched up from $19.17 billion in 2022 to $20.67 billion in 2024. Profitability shot up from $411 million to $3.20 billion. But honestly, this surge on the bottom line is deceptive. I say this because, in 2024, the company booked a $2.92 billion gain on some of its hospitals. Leading up to that point, the company had 61 hospitals. But in 2024, it brought in $4.98 billion on a gross basis from the sale of facilities and other assets. Other profitability metrics are more meaningful. As the chart above illustrates, cash flows for the company have moved generally higher, though 2024 did see some weakness on an operating cash flow basis compared to what the business saw the previous year.

In the chart above, you can also see results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Revenue and cash flows grew across the board. This comes not only as a result of a rise in the number of hospitals that the business has in operation from 49 to 50, but also from some other changes that the company has exhibited. But I will get to that in just a moment.

First, I think that a general description of the performance of its Hospital Operations segment is in order. From 2022 through 2024, revenue associated with this business dipped from $16.12 billion to $16.14 billion. And for this year, the $12.04 billion generated during the first nine months fell short of the $12.33 billion the company reported a year ago. The general weakness that the company has seen during this window of time can be chalked up primarily to the aforementioned asset sale.

In order to better position itself for the future, the company sacrificed near-term revenue. And as a result, the decrease in the size of its physical footprint translated to a decline in overall admissions. Last year, for instance, admissions came in at only 505,828. That's down from the 538,324 reported a year earlier. And adjusted admissions dropped from 983,914 to 914,479. We have seen some recovery in overall admissions for the year. Also, it is worth noting that surgeries have declined as a result of the changes that the company has made to its portfolio.

Last year, surgeries amounted to 307,732. But the year before that, they were 347,156. For this year, the 202,321 surgeries reported by the company fell short of the 204,013 reported last year. Perhaps the only improvement that we saw across the board has been a rise in the utilization rate of its licensed beds. As these declined, and as the company focused on the assets that it decided to keep, the utilization rate expanded from 48.9% in 2022 to 49.6% in 2024. And for the first nine months of this year, the 51.2% utilization rate exceeded the 51% reported a year ago.

Although the Hospital Operations segment for the company has seen revenue drop some, profitability has been on the rise. But the real driver of upside, both from a profitability standpoint and from a revenue standpoint, has been its Ambulatory Care division. Management has made a concerted effort to grow this part of the company. And there's a very good reason why.

Simply put, this part of the business has significant profit margins. As the chart above illustrates, revenue exploded from $3.25 billion in 2022 to $4.53 billion last year. And with that, segment profits jumped from $1.33 billion to $1.81 billion. For the first nine months of this year, the $3.74 billion in revenue, which exceeded the $3.28 billion reported for last year, yielded profits of $1.45 billion. That was up soundly from the $1.28 billion reported a year earlier.

As the chart above illustrates, the company has invested heavily in growing the physical footprint for this part of the business. Back in 2022, the company had only 308 consolidated facilities under this unit. But today, that number stands at 406. With that rise in location count has also come a growth in the number of consolidated cases that the unit has experienced. More cases mean greater revenue, so this kind of expansion is perfectly logical.

Honestly, I am very excited about this part of the business. And that's because management has demonstrated, not only through revenue and profit growth but also through margin growth, that this part of the business is the real driver of value for shareholders. Of the $4.47 billion to $4.57 billion worth of EBITDA the company is forecasting for this year, nearly half of that is expected to come from the Ambulatory Care segment despite the fact that it will be responsible for only about 24.1% of overall revenue. And long-term, the company continues to take advantage of service line additions under this division. In fact, for the first nine months of 2025, it added 211 new service lines. This has helped to fuel same-facility system-wide revenue growth. From 2015 through 2025, the annualized growth rate of same-facility system-wide revenue was about 6.2%.

Simultaneous to capturing this kind of expansion, the company has also been focused on reducing overall leverage. Back in 2017, for instance, the net leverage ratio for the company was 5.86. But through organic growth, growth by means of acquisitions, and the aforementioned hospital asset sale, the firm ultimately reduced this to 2.54 by the end of last year. And as of the third quarter of this year, it stood at 2.30. When we strip out non-controlling interests, we get a decline from 2017 through today from 6.95 to 2.93. And what's really impressive to me is that management has accomplished this while also returning capital to shareholders. In fact, in the first nine months of this year, it has bought back $1.19 billion worth of stock. And with $1.69 billion in buyback capacity remaining, we can expect additional purchases as time goes on.

If we project out results based on management's expectations for this year, and if we also use historical results for the company for the 2024 fiscal year, we can see in the chart above how shares of the business are currently priced. Honestly, these are very low multiples. And this is true not only on an absolute basis but relative to other similar firms also. In that same chart, I compared Tenet Healthcare Corporation to five other similar firms. And what I found was that, across the board, our candidate was cheaper than these other five businesses.

To illustrate for you just how much potential upside might exist here, I need only point you to the chart above. In it, you can see how much upside the stock would experience if it were to trade at the multiple of the lowest of these five competitors. Using the price to operating cash flow approach, we would see upside of 41.8%. And using the EV to EBITDA approach, it would be a more modest 10.2%. And if we use, instead, the average of these five businesses, stripping out the most expensive of the five companies when it comes to the price to operating cash flow multiple since it does appear to be a clear outlier, we would get upside of between 75.9% and 196.5%.

Probably the only thing that I am a bit cautious about when it comes to Tenet Healthcare Corporation would be exposure to changes in the regulatory environment. Unless there are changes that are made, millions of Americans are going to lose their health insurance because of the government spending bill that was passed earlier this year. Medicare will likely be spared from this. But over time, we are looking at a 15% total reduction in federal Medicaid funding thanks to the current administration. In fact, one source estimates that by 2034, as many as 7.5 million people could lose Medicaid coverage compared to if the bill had not been passed. And another 2.1 million will lose access because of the expiration of certain ACA marketplace subsidies.

We don't know the full impact that this will have on a company such as Tenet Healthcare Corporation. But we do know that about 11.7% of the firm's revenue in the latest quarter came from Medicaid. A lot of the company’s money comes from managed care activities. But we know that, included in that, would be those covered under the marketplace. The good news is that only about 7% of consolidated revenue that the business generates comes from those exchanges. But still, this could have some impact on the company even though management has not laid out its own expectations regarding potential pain. The fact that the business is so cheap, however, and the fact that only a sizable minority portion of its revenue comes from sources that we'll see only some reduction in care, makes me feel more confident about the position of the company even in light of this challenge. But investors should absolutely keep an eye on things moving forward.

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, I would argue that Tenet Healthcare Corporation remains a compelling prospect. Admittedly, there are certain risk factors, as I laid out already. But on the whole, this seems to be a healthy company that's trading on the cheap. Management has made great progress in recent years, and that has been reflected in the form of higher revenue, profits, cash flows, and an elevated share price. But still, I think that additional upside is warranted here. So naturally, I have no problem maintaining my ‘buy’ rating on the stock.
